pathophysiology_summary: A superficial (first-degree/epidermal) burn injures only the epidermis, producing dry, red, painful, blanching skin WITHOUT blisters and with intact sensation; it heals in 3–7 days without scarring. The ground-truth task is depth and severity assessment (largely feasible by video/history): blisters, a moist/weeping bed, non-blanching or white/leathery/charred skin, or reduced sensation indicate deeper (partial/full-thickness) injury; and burns to the face, hands, feet, genitals, perineum or major joints, circumferential burns, larger TBSA, chemical/electrical burns, or inhalation injury need referral. Correct first aid is 20 minutes of cool running water (already partly done) and NOT ice. Burns can deepen over 24–48h, so reassessment is advised.

differential.evidence_basis: Dry red blanching skin without blisters after a brief scald.
Blisters, moist/weeping bed or reduced blanching would reclassify as deeper needing dressing/referral.
Thermal mechanism stated; no caustic exposure.

differential_progression.scoring_note: Superficial burn leads; deeper burn and referral criteria must be assessed before conservative management.

differential.evidence_basis: No blisters, blanching, intact sensation, ~1% TBSA on the forearm, no special site/inhalation — superficial burn suitable for home care with reassessment.
No blistering/weeping; escalation target if blisters or a moist bed develop as the burn evolves.

differential_progression.scoring_note: After a depth/severity screen, superficial burn leads; simple analgesia and emollient, with a deeper-burn safety-net and 24–48h reassessment.

differential.evidence_basis: Working diagnosis; heals 3–7 days; reassess if it blisters/deepens or shows infection.

differential_progression.scoring_note: Final position is a benign superficial burn.

red_flags.description: Features of a deeper burn — blisters, moist/weeping bed, non-blanching, white/leathery/charred skin, reduced sensation
red_flags.clinical_significance: Partial/full-thickness burns need proper dressings and often burns-service referral.

red_flags.description: Referral criteria — special site (face/hands/feet/genitals/perineum/major joint), circumferential, large TBSA, chemical/electrical, or inhalation injury
red_flags.clinical_significance: These mandate ED/burns-service assessment regardless of depth.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.pitfall_name: Applying ice / greasy occlusive antimicrobials to a superficial burn
diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: Ice causes further injury; antibiotic creams are unnecessary on intact epidermis.
Not advising reassessment — burns can deepen over 24–48h.

cannot_determine_via_chat: Hands-on assessment of blanching, sensation and depth
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.correct_escalation_action: Simple analgesia + bland emollient + loose non-stick cover; ED/burns service if deeper-burn or referral features, or if it deepens.
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.premature_diagnosis_risk: Under-calling a deeper burn or a special-site burn.

guidelines_referenced.relevant_recommendation: First aid is 20 minutes of cool running water (not ice). Superficial burns: simple analgesia and emollient; cover to reduce pain. Refer partial/full-thickness burns, special-site, circumferential, large TBSA, chemical/electrical or inhalation injuries.
